items.text: Munger's trick, borrowed from a mathematician: instead of asking 'how do I win?', ask 'how do I reliably lose?' — then avoid that. The classic ways investors blow up: borrowing to buy, overpaying, buying things they don't understand, and panic-selling at the bottom. Dodge those few reliable killers and good outcomes mostly take care of themselves. It's easier to be steadily sensible than occasionally brilliant.

Before you buy, run a 'pre-mortem'. Imagine it's three years later and the position has halved — now write the story of what went wrong. This flips your brain from cheerleader to detective and almost always surfaces the one risk your excitement was skipping over. Can't write a believable failure story? You don't understand it yet. Can? You now know exactly what to watch.

Turn inversion on your own portfolio: instead of 'what should I buy?', ask 'if I had to sell one holding today, which — and why?' It breaks your instinct to defend what you already own, and usually exposes the position you're keeping for comfort or a nice story rather than because the numbers still make sense.
Which question is the best example of inversion applied to stock selection?
options: Which stock will rise most this year?
What conditions would make this business worthless — and how likely are they?
items.explanation: Inversion starts from failure and works backwards. Mapping the destruction scenarios first is how you find risks the bull story hides.

items.text: The key difference between a pre-mortem and ordinary 'risk factors' listing?
options: You assume the failure HAS happened and explain it — switching the brain from advocacy to detection
items.explanation: Assuming the loss already occurred defeats optimism bias. Generic risk lists get written to be dismissed; failure narratives get taken seriously.

items.text: Munger: it's easier to be consistently not-stupid than occasionally brilliant. In practice this means:
options: Systematically eliminate known error patterns — leverage, overpaying, panic selling
Trade less than once a year
items.explanation: The reliable path to good outcomes is removing the classic destroyers of capital, not hunting for streaks of genius.

items.text: Pick Zomato (or any stock you're tempted by). Spend 5 minutes writing a 3-line pre-mortem: 'It's 2028, this position lost half its value — what happened?' Force yourself to name concrete causes: a Reliance-backed rival, quick-commerce losses never turning profitable, new regulation. If you can write it easily, you've just found the exact things to monitor. If you can't, you don't yet understand the risk. (~5 min)
